items.q: How do you say 'I am going to Spain' in French?
items.why: Students confuse aller (to go - movement away from speaker) and venir (to come - movement towards speaker). 'Je vais' = I'm going; 'je viens' = I'm coming. Also: en + feminine country, au + masculine, aux + plural.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I am going to go to Spain'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Je vais aller en Espagne.. “Je suis allé en Espagne.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which preposition is correct: 'Je voyage ___ avion'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: en. “par” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many endings does the French imperfect tense have (one per person)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 endings. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
